source_metadata.title_raw: Carl K. Newton - A-82-114 - June 24, 1982 - Los Angeles

content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

January 24, 1983


John D. Jorgenson
Office of the City Attorney
City of Menlo Park
1100 Alma Street
Menlo Park, CA 94205

Re: Advice Request on Behalf of
Councilmembers Billy Ray While
and Matt Lehmann, Our Advice
No. A-82-214

Dear Mr. Jorgenson:

Thank you for your letter requesting our advice on behalf of
two Menlo Park City Councilmembers, Billy Ray White and Matt
Lehmann. You have presented the issues to be analyzed as
follows:

FACTS

Councilmember Billy Ray White's wife is employed by Stanford
University Hospital, a non-profit corporation governed by a
Board of Directors appointed by the Board of Trustees of Leland
Stanford Junior University.

Councilmember Mat Lehmann has an appointment as a lecturer
in engineering for Stanford University.

The City of Palo Alto is currently processing an application
by Stanford University to rezone University Lands to
permit construction of 1469 housing units (The "Project")
adjacent to the boundary line between Menlo Park and Palo Alto
at that point. Many residents of the City of Menlo Park have
expressed serious concerns about the proposal and many fear that
the project would have a severe negative effect on Menlo Park.
Menlo Park's City Council will make a recommendation to
Palo Alto concerning this matter in the near future.

John D. Jorgenson
January 24, 1983
Page 2


     The project is located along Willow Road.  Stanford
University has indicated its willingness and desire to construct
an extension of Willow Road at its own expense and the Menlo
Park City County is considering the making of a recommendation
to Palo Alto concerning this matter.

     Thus, there are two issues before the Menlo Park City
Council, neither of which is under the direct control of Menlo
Park, but both of which have an important impact on Menlo Park.
One may fairly assume that the City Council of Palo Alto, which
does control the two decisions, will give great weight to the
considered views of the Menlo Park Council.  Also, some of the
mitigations described in the EIR which are designed to alleviate
negative impacts caused by the project can only be accomplished
by action taken by the City Council of the City of Menlo Park.

                            QUESTIONS

     You have asked us to answer the following questions.

     (a)  Does Mr. White have a financial interest under
Government Code Section 87103(c) if the source of income
consists of his community property interest in his wife's
salary?

     (b)  Is the "governmental decision" (Under Government
Code Section 87100) the decision to advise the Palo Alto
Planning Commission and City Council of the views of Menlo
Park?  Or is the "decision" the resolution of the issues now
before the Palo Alto bodies?

     (c)  Does the determination of either (or both) of the
two issues before the Menlo Park City Council have a
material financial effect on Stanford under 2 Adm. Code
Section 18702?  [It is probably impossible at this time to
determine with any certainty, what the effect on Stanford
will be of either of the two decisions under the standard
set by 2 Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3).] If the answer to
this question is yes, would action on either (or both) of
these matters by White and/or Lehmann be prohibited under
Government Code Section 87100, et seq.?

     (d)  Would Stanford University constitute a "predominant
industry" within the Councilmember's jurisdiction, and
thereby a "significant segment of the public generally," as
it is described in 2 Adm. Code Section 18703(b)?

John D. Jorgenson
January 24, 1983
Page 3


ANALYSIS

Generally, the Political Reform Act1/ requires that a public
official not participate in making, and not use his or
her official position to influence a decision in which he or she
has a financial interest. Section 87100. Under Section 87103:

An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

* * *

(c) Any source of income, ... aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

a. Mr. White's Source of Income

With respect to your question (a), Stanford is a source of
income to Mr. White in excess of $250, since we can safely
assume that Mrs. White receives more than $500 in income from
Stanford. Section 82030(a) states, in part:

"Income" means ... a payment received, including but
not limited to any salary, wage ... and including any
community property interest in income of a spouse....

Thus, one-half of Mrs. White's salary from Stanford is
attributable to Mr. White.

Although you did not specifically ask about Mr. Lehmann, it
is clear that Stanford is a source of income to Mr. Lehmann
within the meaning of Section 87103(c) and he is an employee of
Stanford under Section 87103(d).

_______________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. All statutory
references made are to the Government Code unless otherwise
specified.

John D. Jorgenson
January 24, 1983
Page 4


       D.   What is the Governmental Decision?

       Government Code Section 87100 prohibits an official from
making a decision, or from participating in a decision, in which
he has a financial interest. There are several governmental
decisions involved here. Palo Alto is clearly going to make a
decision on Stanford's zoning application. The Menlo Park City
Council is also clearly going to have significant input into the
decision to be made by the officials of neighboring Palo Alto.
Menlo Park will make a governmental decision as to what position
to take on Stanford's proposal. By taking a position, it will
be participating in the making of Palo Alto's governmental
decision. Section 87100. (See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700
and advice letter to Scott Keene, No. A-81-513, December 30,
1981, copy enclosed.)

       Your letter also indicates that Menlo Park's cooperation
will be required as to certain aspects of the project if it is
to go forward. Menlo Park will have to make a governmental
decision to cooperate, or not cooperate, with the project.

       C.   Material Financial Effect

       Your letter states that it is not possible to determine at
this time whether the effects of the zoning decision [project
proceeding or not proceeding] will increase or decrease
Stanford's current assets by $100,000 or more. You have asked
in your question "c" whether there will be a material financial
effect "under the standard set by 2 [Cal.] Adm. Code Section
18702(b)(3)".

       Previous advice letter requests regarding Stanford have
advised us that Stanford University is not a business entity2/
because it is a tax-exempt, non-profit organization under
Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. Consequently,
the provisions of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(C) do not
apply and the $100,000 standard is inapplicable. For a non-
business entity source of income, the correct test is enunciated
in Section 18702(b)(3)(D).3/

_____________________

       2/   Section 82005 defines "business entity" as "any
organization or enterprise operated for profit...."

       3/   2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(D) states:

            If the source of income is not a business entity, the
       decision will have a significant effect on the source.

John D. Jorgenson
January 24, 1983
Page 5


     While your letter is somewhat vague as to the effect on
Stanford, it seems logical to us that approval to permit the
construction of 1469 housing units on the Stanford campus
(which, we are advised, is suffering a housing shortage) will be
significant both in terms of capital costs and in terms of the
effect on Stanford's ability to provide housing for its students
and faculty. The impact of the second decision, relating to the
extension of Willow Road, while not as clear, still appears to
be significant, considering the cost of such construction at
today's prices, and the success of the housing project is
apparently involved with the extension. Therefore, we conclude
that, as to both of the pending decisions, there is a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect upon Stanford.

     d. Predominant Industry

     You have asked whether Stanford University constitutes a
"predominant industry" in the jurisdiction in question within
the meaning of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703(b). If Stanford
were such a "predominant industry" then it would constitute a
significant segment of the public generally in the jurisdiction,
and there would be no conflict.

     We see no basis for determining that Stanford University is
a "predominant industry" within the meaning of Section
18703(b). First, Stanford is not even located within the
jurisdiction of Menlo Park or within the districts from which
Messrs. White and Lehmann were elected. Second, Stanford is but
one entity/ and is not in and of itself an industry, trade or
profession.2/ Even if we were to treat Stanford as being a
"profession" or "industry", the profession or industry would be
education. However, there is no evidence that education is a
predominant industry or profession in Menlo Park. On these
facts, we must conclude that Stanford is not a significant
segment of the public generally under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18703(b).

     _________________________

     4/ Stanford is not a business entity (supra), therefore
it is difficult to reconcile how a non-profit entity could be
considered an "industry".

     5/ We are not sure that one employer could ever
constitute a predominant industry, trade or profession. Several
employers in a jurisdiction where that type of industry
predominates is what Section 18703(b) contemplates.

John D. Jorgenson
January 24, 1983
Page 6


CONCLUSION

     Messrs. White and Lehmann must disqualify themselves from
participating in the decisions by the Melo Park City Council on
the rezone and on the extension of Willow Road.5/  The
question of disqualification on possible future decisions on
mitigation measures will have to be evaluated at the time when
the measures are considered, upon the basis of the information
available at that time.

     Should you have any questions, please feel free to call me
at 916/322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert E. Leidign
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

REL:km
Enclosures




_________________
     5/  As the court stated in Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.
App. 3d 817, 823:

     Any employee, in the private or public sector, wishes
to keep his job and maintain good relations with his
employer. A person who must make decisions which may affect
his employer's purse is in a situation where he may not give
full consideration to the merits of the decision.

BILLY RAY WHITE
MAYOR

PIG GUNN
MAYOR PRO TEM

MARTY BERNARD
COUNCILMEMBER

JACK H. MORRIS
COUNCILMEMBER

KAY PAAK
COUNCILMEMBER

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY

1100 ALMA STREET / MENLO PARK, CALIFORNIA 94025 / TELEPHONE (415) 324-9300

December 23, 1982


State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804

Re:  Conflict of Interest

     I respectfully request advice or an opinion pursuant
to Government Code §83114, regarding two Menlo Park City
Councilmembers' duties under Government Code Section
87100.

The Decision

     (a)  The City of Palo Alto is currently processing
an application by Stanford University to zone for (rezoned)
University lands to permit construction of 1469 housing
units (The "Project").  The project is called "Stanford
West".  The project is adjacent to San Francisquito
Creek, the boundary line between Menlo Park and Palo Alto
at that point.  Many residents of the City of Menlo Park
have expressed serious concerns about the proposal and
many fear that the project would have a severe negative
effect on Menlo Park.  Menlo Park's City Council will
make a recommendation to Palo Alto concerning this matter
in the near future.

     (b)  The project is located along Willow Road.
Willow Road in Palo Alto has its Eastern terminus at the
Stanford Shopping Center and does not directly connect
into El Camino Real.  The attached Figure 1 from the
Draft EIR shows a Willow Road connection to El Camino
Real.  Stanford University has indicated its willingness
and desire to construct this link to El Camino Real at
its own expense and the Menlo Park City Council is
considering the making of a recommendation to Palo Alto
concerning this matter.

     (c)  Thus, there are two issues before the Menlo
Park City Council, neither of which is under the direct

Page Two

control of Menlo Park, but both of which have an
important impact on Menlo Park. One may fairly assume
that the City Council of Palo Alto which does control the
two decisions, will give great weight to the considered
views of the Menlo Park Council. Also, some of the
mitigations described in the EIR which are designed to
alleviate negative impacts caused by the project can only
be accomplished by action taken by the City Council of
the City of Menlo Park.

It is not possible to determine at this time
whether Stanford University's current assets will be
increased or decreased by $100,000.00 or more as the
result of the project.

The Players

(a) Councilmember Billy Ray White is married to
Zerlene White. She is employed as a material assistant
by Stanford University Hospital, a non-profit corporation
governed by a Board of Directors appointed by the Board
of Trustees of Leland Stanford Junior University. For
these purposes, the Stanford Hospital should be
considered as one and the same with Stanford University.
Mrs. White's position is not in any way related to the
management of the hospital or Stanford University. The
use or development of Stanford University lands or lands
surrounding Stanford University has not, and most likely
will not, in any way have a financial effect or impact on
Mrs. White's income from the hospital.

(b) Councilmember Matt Lehmann has an appointment
as a lecturer in engineering for Stanford University.
The land use decisions regarding the University or its
lands would not in any way have a financial effect or
impact on his income from the University.

(c) Neither official (White nor Lehmann) believes
that his existing relationship with Stanford will inter-
fere with the performance of his duties free from bias
and there is neither a financial benefit accruing to the
Councilmember as a result of the decision, nor is there a
nexus between the decision and the Councilmember's (or
his wife's) job with the University.

The Questions

a) Does Mr. White have a financial interest under
Government Code §87103(c) if the source of
income consists of his community property
interest in his wife's salary?

Page Three

b)    Is the "governmental decision" (under Government Code §87100) the decision to advise the Palo Alto Planning Commission and City Council of the views of Menlo Park?  Or is the "decision" the resolution of the issues now before the Palo Alto bodies?

c)    Does the determination of either (or both) of the two issues before the Menlo Park City Council have a material financial effect on Stanford under 2 Adm. Code §18702?  [It is probably impossible at this time to determine with any certainty, what the effect on Stanford will be of either of the two decisions under the standard set by 2 Adm. Code §18702 (b) (3).] If the answer to this question is yes, would action on either (or both) of these matters by White and/or Lehmann be prohibited under Government Code Section 87100 et. seq.?

d)    Would Stanford University constitute a "predominant industry" within the Council-members' jurisdiction, and thereby a "significant segment of the public generally," as it is described in 2 Adm. Code §18703(b)?

Kindly advise if you will be willing to respond to these inquiries and if so, when such a response shall take place.  If you need further information, please advise.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        John D. Jorgenson

JDJ/dp
Attachment

QUESTION: You have asked us to answer the following questions. 
(a) 
Does Mr. White have a financial interest under 
Government Code Section 87103(c) if the source of income 
consists of his community property interest in his wife's 
salary? 
(b) 
Is the "governmental decision" (Under Government 
Code Section 87100) the decision to advise the Palo Alto 
Planning Commission and City Council of the views of Menlo 
Park? 
Or is the "decision" the resolution of the issues now 
before the Palo Alto bodies? 
(c) 
Does the determination of either (or both) of the 
two issues before the Menlo Park City Council have a 
material financial effect on Stanford under 2 Adm. Code 
Section 18702? 
[It is probably impossible at this time to 
determine with any certainty, what the effect on Stanford 
will be of either of the two decisions under the standard 
set by 2 Adm. Code Section 18702(b) (3).] 
If the answer to 
this question is yes, would action on either (or both) of 
these matters by White and/or Lehmann be prohibited under 
Government Code Section 87100, et seq.? 
(d) 
Would Stanford University constitute a "predominant 
industry" within the Councilmember's jurisdiction, and 
thereby a "significant segment of the public generally," as 
it is described in 2 Adm. Code Section 18703(b)? 


John D. Jorgenson 
January 24, 1983

CONCLUSION: Mssrs. White and Lehmann must disqualify themselves from 
participating in the decisions by the Menlo Park City Council on 
the rezone and on the extension of Willow Road.~/ The 
question of disqualification on possible future decisions on 
mitigation measures will have to be evaluated at the time when 
the measures are considered, upon the basis of the information 
available at that time. 
Should you nave any questions, please feel free to call me 
at 916/322-5901.
